channel.title: Derek Wakefield
channel.description: Assistant Professor of Political Science, Bucknell University

item.title: The Coffee Shop
item.dc:creator: Derek Wakefield
item.description:
A small voice in the dark moves more than it knows. Chirp anyway.

Imagine a coffee shop that opened in 1980 and never closed.

Every chair is a sociologist or political scientist. Some have been there since the doors opened — Coleman in one corner, Putnam at the bar, Massey by the window watching people walk past. Others arrived later — Mendelberg ordered her first espresso in the mid-90s and has been here ever since. The newest baristas just clocked in last year.

The shop has 7,000 chairs and they’re all full.

Most conversations are citations — someone overhearing what someone else said and writing it down. But some conversations are something rarer: the kind where one scholar trains another, where a sentence in 1983 ends up shaping a sentence in 2018 because the second person learned how to think from the first.

This page is a map of those conversations.

What you’re looking at

  • 7,000 dots. Each dot is an author who published in a flagship sociology or political-science journal between 1980 and 2025.
  • Vertical position = the year of their first publication. 1980 at the top, 2025 at the bottom. Scroll down to walk forward through time.
  • Color = field & tier. Reds for sociology, blues for political science. Darker = top-flight journal.
  • Lines connecting dots = mentor → mentee edges. Two kinds:
    • Bold purple lines are Wikipedia-verified advisor/student pairs from the top-500 authors’ biographies. These are gold-standard data.
    • Thin gray lines are inferred via co-authorship asymmetry (Sinatra et al. 2016 Science) — pairs with a ≥5-year first-publication gap who co-authored together in the junior’s early career window.
  • Big black-outlined dots are “heads of school” with 25+ descendants in the graph. There are 84 of them.

Click any dot to isolate that scholar’s intellectual lineage — mentors in purple flowing up, mentees in green flowing down. The decade sidebar on the right summarizes who was training whom in each ten-year slice.

Note

The interaction lives in the embedded viz below. If you want a wider canvas, open it in a new tab.

A note on missingness

This isn’t the whole coffee shop. A few caveats baked in:

  • Anglophone journals only. Bourdieu doesn’t have a chair here. Habermas doesn’t either.
  • Co-authorship inference misses solo influence. Coleman, Granovetter, and Tilly — three of the heaviest hitters in the room — have famous solo papers that trained whole generations through reading, not co-writing. Their footprint here looks smaller than their actual influence.
  • Recent students are sparse. Famous mentors like Mendelberg have 40+ named PhD students on their CVs, but many of those advisees haven’t yet accumulated enough citations to land in the top-7000 universe. They’ll show up in this graph in 5–10 years as their work compounds.

The viz makes this gap visible rather than hiding it. Click any famous-but-childless author and you’ll see the citation-vs-genealogy callout explaining why.

The viz

Method, briefly

stage source tool
Authors + citations + journals OpenAlex Works API curated 87-journal whitelist, top-cited works 1980-2025
Inferred mentor edges OpenAlex co-author pairs in same whitelist Sinatra-style asymmetry rule (≥5yr first-pub gap, early-career joint paper)
Verified mentor edges Wikipedia infobox fields (doctoral_students, doctoral_advisor, notable_students) on top-500 authors parse + match to OpenAlex universe
CV-verified edges (rolling) Direct CV scrapes for non-Wikipedia authors regex on “DISSERTATION ADVISING” / “PhD Students” sections, role-tagged (chair / cochair / committee)
Layout width-weighted DFS tree rooted at earliest-mentor per component; Y = first publication year; tilt-fold for over-wide subtrees Python + NetworkX + Plotly Scattergl

The whole pipeline is resumable. Every time I sit at the coffee shop I add a few more chairs.

Latest session — 2026-05-10

A few more chairs, pulled in by hand.

NoteWhat got added today

16 CV-verified mentor → mentee edges, sourced from CVs, lab pages, and dissertation pages of an initial 17-scholar Latino-politics roster plus three calibration anchors (Mendelberg, Meier, Pérez). Sample edges:

  • Tali Mendelberg → Claire Willeck (chair, Princeton). Diss: Active Civics: How Civic Education Shapes Political Engagement. Won the 2024 APSA E. E. Schattschneider Award. Committee: Mendelberg, Markus Prior, Andy Guess, Rocio Titiunik.
  • Efrén Pérez → 5 current PhDs in REPS Lab at UCLA: Ramona Alhambra, Gustavo A. Mártir Luna, Kasheena G. Rogbeer, Sydney Tran, Tricia Huynh.
  • Michael Jones-Correa → Angie Ocampo-Roland (chair, Penn 2021) — inferred, pending CV verification. The pipeline’s confidence column was built for this.

One correction worth flagging. First-pass surface search guessed Jason Casellas as Sam Chapa’s dissertation chair at Houston (overlapping topic, same department, plausible). Her actual chair is Lydia Tiede. The verification pass caught it from her dissertation page. This is exactly the failure mode the pipeline’s audit step is designed to catch — topic similarity is not the same as supervision.

187 dissertation-cited authors extracted from the Worked Cited section of my dissertation. 12 of the 17 Phase-1 roster members appear in it; 25 cited-3×-or-more authors are Tier-2 promotion candidates (Barreto leads at 8 citations, then Segura at 6, Masuoka and Pérez at 5).

None of this is in the viz yet — it sits in the rolling CV-verified-edges queue. The next rebuild will route these names through OpenAlex resolution and fold them into the graph. Same coffee shop, a few more chairs.

TipExpansion crawl — 2026-05-10 (US poli-sci / public-policy universe)

Sat down at the coffee shop and counted everyone in the room. The filter: US-affiliated authors with i10-index ≥ 2 (two or more works each with ten or more citations) publishing in any of 121 OpenAlex topics under the subfields “Political Science and International Relations” + “Public Administration.”

The crawl pulled 43,953 unique authors. Three filter tiers, each useful for different things:

Filter tier Authors What it captures What it’s good for
Broad 43,423 i10≥2, US, any of 121 poli-sci-adjacent topics, junk institutions removed Maximum recall — includes cross-discipline scholars who publish on political topics
Subfield-proxy 20,466 Same, but top topic must be in PoliSci/IR/PoliSci+Soc/PubAdmin subfields Reasonable middle — close to the original estimate (~17K)
Concept-tight 9,724 Same, but top concept must be Politics / Political science / Public administration / IR Highest precision — the top 15 by h-index are all actual political scientists

Top of the concept-tight list (sanity check): Francis Fukuyama, Robert Keohane, Pippa Norris, John C. Campbell, Kenneth J. Meier, Barry Bozeman, B. Guy Peters, Theda Skocpol, Donald P. Moynihan, Robert S. Erikson, Shanto Iyengar, John W. Meyer, Sidney Tarrow, Michael W. Apple. That’s the right neighborhood.

Top institutions in the concept-tight slice: Yale (87), Columbia (81), Wisconsin–Madison (76), UNC (73), UT Austin (73), American (71), Georgetown (71), NYU (70), Stanford (69), GW (69), Ohio State (67), Princeton (66), Cornell (66), Duke (65), Penn State (65), UCLA (62), Rutgers (62), Hopkins (61), Vanderbilt (58), Maryland (56), Washington (55), Michigan State (53), George Mason (52), Florida State (51).

i10-index distribution. Roughly 53% sit at the 2–5 bar (the entry tier; established but not heavily-cited). 18% are 6–10. 14% are 11–20. 11% are 21–50. The top 1% (418 authors) have i10 > 100 — the field-defining figures the existing viz’s “Wikipedia top-500” layer is built on.

Caveats baked in. OpenAlex’s author-disambiguation is uneven — affiliations like “Harvard University Press” attributed to Theda Skocpol or “Dartmouth–Hitchcock Medical Center” to Donald P. Green are merge errors that haven’t been fixed at the source. The pipeline can scrub these by matching against IPEDS / Carnegie institution lists in a post-process pass. Also: 530 records had “junk institutions” (Twitter (United States), Film Independent, Independent Researcher) — those were dropped at the clean tier.

What this enabled. The Coffee Shop originally showed ~7,000 dots filtered to a 87-journal flagship whitelist. The expansion crawl added authors who publish in venues outside that whitelist but still meet the citation-impact bar. After deduping against the existing universe and dropping authors whose first publication was before 1980, the viz below now plots ~13,200 dots — up from 7,000. The 84 heads-of-school (≥25 descendants) are preserved; the new authors mostly land as isolates in the right-margin grid, sorted by their first-publication year.

What still needs work. New authors don’t yet have inferred mentor edges — pulling co-authorship asymmetry for each requires another pipeline pass (stages 03–04). For the moment they’re standalone dots; their lines come in the next rebuild.

Flagged for soon, not now. The non-US universe — UK + Canada + Australia (~half the US count), Continental Europe (~similar magnitude), Latin America (~smaller), East Asia / South Asia / Africa (varied indexing quality). Going global multiplies the count by roughly 2.5–3×.

Built with Claude. Re-framed as a coffee shop because the actual phrase “co-authorship-asymmetry-inferred mentorship network” makes most people stop reading at “co-”.

item.title: Wor Gems
item.description:
A WarPips-inspired isometric auto-battler. Pick a banner — Tonric Concord, Wildfire Ascension, or Renkindled Mist — and deploy soldiers, miners, snipers, tanks, mechs, helicopters, and airplanes into a destructible battlefield. Anti-air units engage AIR; ground units stay in their lane. Cover blocks 50% of incoming damage, but every prop on the field is destructible. Projectiles track their targets. Each unit type has its own idle animation.

Built on top of the Gem in Eyes engine canon. Lore-anchored in the bobology.

Download and open locally — no internet required.

⬇   Download HTML    or open in browser →

Controls: Q W E R T Y U deploy units · click cards to deploy · units auto-engage at range · seek cover when fighting · AA-flagged units engage AIR.

Factions:

  • Tonric Concord — heavy, durable, arrives. HP +15%, SPD −15%.
  • Wildfire Ascension — glass cannon. ATK +20%, SPD +25%, HP −15%.
  • Renkindled Mist — long-shot, sustain. RNG +10%, COST −5%.

Balance vetted against 10,000 AI-vs-AI matches; faction winrates landed at 50% / 41% / 59%.

item.title: Mind Palace
item.description:
A research-grade visualization built around CES (Cooperative Election Study) data, packaged as one self-contained HTML page. The companion Python pipeline shapes the data; the page renders the result.

Download and open locally — no internet connection required after download.

⬇   Download HTML    or open in browser →

item.title: Gem in Eyes — an introduction
item.description:
A small light in a deep room. A cube of stone with five lit corners and one quiet pocket at the center. You hum, and the room hums back. Five gods — Lik, Ton, Ren, Nif, Eye — keep the corners lit.

This piece is a self-contained HTML file. Download it and open it locally in any browser.

⬇   Download HTML    or open in browser →
